chapter_context.summary: Pronounces the doom of the great maritime merchant city of Tyre for celebrating Jerusalem's ruin, prophesying that Nebuchadnezzar will lay her waste, and she will become a bare rock for drying fishing nets.

outline.text: Tyre's Prideful Rejoicing over the Fall of Jerusalem
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Tyre's Prideful Rejoicing over the Fall of Jerusalem (26:1-6), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Ezekiel.

outline.text: Nebuchadnezzar's Conquest: Tyre Made a Bare Rock for Drying Nets
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Nebuchadnezzar's Conquest: Tyre Made a Bare Rock for Drying Nets (26:7-14), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Ezekiel.

outline.text: Shaking of Isles and Consignment of Tyre to Pit of Dead
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Shaking of Isles and Consignment of Tyre to Pit of Dead (26:15-21), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Ezekiel.

key_themes.title: Prophecies Against Pagan Nations
key_themes.description: This chapter forms a core part of Ezekiel's prophecies concerning prophecies against pagan nations, illustrating God's absolute sovereignty in both justice and final restoration.

key_themes.title: The Glory of the Lord
key_themes.description: A central theological theme in Ezekiel, describing the overwhelming majesty of God's presence, its departure due to sin, and its return in future restoration.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the visions, symbolic actions, and warnings of Chapter 26, what does this teach you about God's holiness and His desire for deep, covenant integrity in your own life today?
How does this chapter encourage you to rely on the Spirit's empowerment to stand on your feet and witness faithfully in a challenging culture?

cultural_historical.title: The Exile in Babylon
cultural_historical.text: Ezekiel ministered in exile among the deportees by the River Chebar, providing a crucial priestly voice that helped preserve Israel's faith during the collapse of the monarchy and the destruction of the Temple.
